title: Brain Tumours — Primary & Metastatic
oneLiner: Space-occupying lesions (primary or metastatic) causing focal neurology, raised ICP, or seizures — treatable but high-risk for herniation.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Uncal herniation (transtentorial):** Pupil asymmetry (ipsilateral fixed/dilated) + progressive coma + contralateral hemiparesis. *If no time for imaging: give mannitol or hyperventilate.*  
- **Seizure with status epilepticus:** Prolonged convulsions >5 min or without return to baseline. *Treat immediately with lorazepam/ midazolam – don't wait for MRI.*  
- **Intratumoural haemorrhage (especially melanoma/RCC metastases):** Sudden catastrophic headache + vomiting + GCS drop. *Avoid LP – get CT head stat.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Headache:** Worse in morning, with coughing/bending, new-onset in cancer patient  
- **Focal neurological deficit:** Hemiparesis, aphasia, visual field cut – correlates with lesion location  
- **Seizures:** Focal onset +/- secondary generalisation – up to 50% of primary brain tumours  
- **Raised ICP signs:** Nausea/vomiting, papilloedema, bradycardia + hypertension (Cushing’s triad late)  
- **Cognitive change:** Confusion, personality change, slow thinking (especially frontal lobe)  
- **New-onset epilepsy in an adult:** This IS brain tumour until proven otherwise
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Brain abscess:** Ring-enhancing lesion + fever + sepsis history → differentiate by diffusion-weighted MRI + blood cultures  
- **Stroke (ischaemic/haemorrhagic):** Acute onset <24h vs progressive; CT head without contrast key  
- **Subdural haematoma:** Often chronic, fluctuating consciousness, history of falls/anticoagulation  
- **Meningioma (common benign):** Extra-axial, dural-based, slow growth – often incidental  
- **Primary CNS lymphoma:** Periventricular, uniform enhancement, often HIV+ / immunosuppressed  
- **Metastases from unknown primary:** Multiple lesions, supra/infratentorial; search for lung/breast/melanoma/RCC/colorectal  
- **Splitter:** In a cancer patient with a single brain lesion, the top differentials are solitary metastasis vs brain abscess – use diffusion-weighted MRI (restricted diffusion suggests abscess).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Blood glucose (fingerstick) to rule out hypoglycaemia causing focal signs  
  - Neurological exam: NIHSS (if stroke mimic suspected)  
  - Visual fields by confrontation, pupil reactivity, fundoscopy (papilloedema if ICP high)  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, calcium, coagulation screen  
  - ESR/CRP (elevated in abscess)  
  - If suspected primary: tumour markers (AFP, β-hCG, CEA, CA125? – but not urgent)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** CT head without contrast (for acute presentation, rule out haemorrhage/hermiation)  
  - **Second-line:** MRI brain with gadolinium (characterise lesion, differentiate abscess, see oedema) – do not delay if stable  
  - Consider CT chest/abdomen/pelvis if no known primary (for metastases)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - EEG if unexplained seizure or non-convulsive status suspected  
  - Biopsy (stereotactic) – definitive diagnosis if single lesion, no known primary, or planned for resection  
  - **Never-miss test:** CT head or MRI brain with contrast in ANY adult with new-onset seizure – failing to image delays treatment of a potentially curable cause.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Secure airway if GCS <8 or signs of herniation  
  - If suspected raised ICP: elevate head 30°, avoid fluids that lower osmolality, give **dexamethasone 10-16 mg IV/PO stat** (reduces vasogenic oedema)  
  - For seizures: **lorazepam 2-4 mg IV** or **midazolam 5-10 mg buccal**; load with **levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV** (max 4-5 g) if in status  
  - Pain management: paracetamol 1 g IV/PO – avoid opioids that mask neurology  
  - **DO NOT give mannitol or hyperventilate unless imminent herniation – only under senior guidance**  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Continue dexamethasone taper (e.g. 16 mg daily in 2-4 divided doses, reduce by 50% every 48h) – monitor for hyperglycaemia, GI bleeding, psychosis  
  - Anti-epileptic drugs (AEDs) only if seizure occurred or at high risk (tumours near motor cortex, melanoma) – levetiracetam 500-1000 mg BD  
  - VTE prophylaxis: **enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily** (risk of haemorrhage? discuss with neurosurgery – many units start 24-48h post-craniotomy)  
  - NBM only if vomiting or awaiting surgery – otherwise encourage oral intake  
  - Arrange MRI brain with contrast, neurosurgical/oncology referral  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Definitive treatment depends on histology: surgical resection, radiotherapy (stereotactic or whole-brain), chemotherapy (e.g. temozolomide for glioblastoma, targeted therapy for EGFR/ALK in NSCLC mets)  
  - For multiple metastases: WBRT or SRS – discuss in MDT  
  - Palliative care referral if poor prognosis or progressive disease  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline 1 L/8h (avoid dextrose if on steroids – hyperglycaemia)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (after discussion with NSx)  
  - Diet: free/puree if swallowing safe; NBM if vomiting or pre-op  
  - Observations: 4-hourly neuro obs (GCS, pupil size, limb power) for first 24h then 6-hourly if stable – escalate if GCS drop ≥2
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Dexamethasone (first-line for oedema):** 10-16 mg IV/PO stat, then 4 mg PO/IV QDS (or 16 mg daily divided). Taper over 2-4 weeks. [Check local policy for taper schedule].  
  - **Key contraindications:** Active GI bleed, untreated infection, systemic fungal infection  
  - **Interactions:** NSAIDs → ↑GI bleeding risk; anticoagulants → ↑bleeding; antidiabetics → hyperglycaemia  
  - **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None significant – but monitor blood glucose (steroid-induced diabetes)  
  - **Most dangerous dose error:** *Abruptly stopping dexamethasone* after >1 week → adrenal crisis – always taper.  
- **Levetiracetam (for seizures):** 500-1000 mg PO/IV BD; loading dose 60 mg/kg (max 4.5 g) if status.  
  - **Key contraindications:** Hypersensitivity  
  - **Interactions:** Minimal – good choice in brain tumour patients (low hepatic metabolism)  
  - **Renal adjustment:** CrCl <50 → reduce dose; check [local policy] for exact CrCl-based dose  
  - **Most dangerous dose error:** *Giving phenytoin as first-line* in brain tumour – risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome, drug interactions with steroids. Use levetiracetam unless contraindicated.  
- **Mannitol (for herniation only):** 0.25-1 g/kg IV over 20 min. [Check local policy].  
- **Lorazepam IV / Midazolam buccal:** as per local guidelines for seizures – beware respiratory depression.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Brain herniation (especially if LP performed with mass effect – fatal)  
  - Seizures (focal or generalised)  
  - Hydrocephalus (if tumour near aqueduct/4th ventricle)  
  - Venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE) – very high risk due to procoagulant state + steroids  
  - Steroid side effects: hyperglycaemia, GI bleed, psychosis, proximal myopathy  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Cognitive decline from tumour or radiotherapy  
  - Radiation necrosis (months-years post RT) – mimics recurrence on MRI  
  - Leptomeningeal disease (seeding via CSF)  
  - Endocrine deficits (if tumour/RT involves hypothalamus-pituitary)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Metastatic:** Median survival 3-12 months depending on primary and performance status (KPS >70 → longer). Surgical resection of single met + SRS improves OS.  
- **Primary glioblastoma:** Median survival 12-15 months with Stupp protocol (surgery + RT + temozolomide).  
- **Meningioma:** Good – 5-year survival >90% if benign and completely resected.  
- **Incidental low-grade glioma:** Watchful – survival often >5 years; but may transform.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check visual fields** – even subtle hemianopia gives you the lesion side and confirms posterior circulation involvement.  
- **Send urgent MRI brain with contrast** in any cancer patient with new headache + focal neurology – don’t wait for CT.  
- **Prescribe a stool softener** (e.g. lactulose 15 mL BD) when starting dexamethasone – prevents constipation which increases ICP.  
- **Arrange a social work/occupational therapy review** early – brain tumours cause functional disability that may need discharge planning.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT perform lumbar puncture** if there is any suggestion of mass effect on CT – risk of tonsillar herniation is fatal. Always get imaging first.  
- **Do NOT give heparin or LMWH** for VTE prophylaxis without discussing with neurosurgery – risk of intratumoural haemorrhage. If on therapeutic anticoagulation (e.g. for PE), bridge with IV unfractionated heparin only after NSx approval.  
- **Do NOT give opioids for headache** without first imaging – they mask neurology and cause respiratory depression. Use paracetamol + dexamethasone.  
- **Do NOT stop steroids abruptly** – taper over 2-4 weeks to avoid adrenal insufficiency and rebound oedema.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening headache not relieved by paracetamol or dexamethasone  
  - New focal weakness, speech difficulty, or vision change  
  - Prolonged (>5 min) seizure or more than 2 seizures in 24 hours  
  - Vomiting (suggests raised ICP)  
  - Suspected spinal cord compression (back pain + leg weakness/urinary retention)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Neurosurgery clinic within 1-2 weeks (or sooner if pre-op)  
  - Oncology referral for definitive treatment planning (SRS, WBRT, chemo)  
  - If no known primary: urgent CT CAP and referral to lung/breast/general oncology MDT
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) immediately if:**  
  - GCS drops by ≥2 points  
  - New pupillary asymmetry or non-reactive pupil  
  - Seizure lasting >5 min (status) – call anaesthetics/ICU  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4 mmol/L  
  - Sudden severe headache with neck stiffness (suggestive of SAH from intratumoural bleed)  
- **Call crash team / ICU if:**  
  - GCS <8 (airway compromise)  
  - Cushing’s reflex (bradycardia + hypertension + irregular respirations)  
  - Any seizure in a patient with known raised ICP – may herniate

rawMarkdown:
## Brain Tumours — Primary & Metastatic
**One-liner:** Space-occupying lesions (primary or metastatic) causing focal neurology, raised ICP, or seizures — treatable but high-risk for herniation.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Uncal herniation (transtentorial):** Pupil asymmetry (ipsilateral fixed/dilated) + progressive coma + contralateral hemiparesis. *If no time for imaging: give mannitol or hyperventilate.*  
- **Seizure with status epilepticus:** Prolonged convulsions >5 min or without return to baseline. *Treat immediately with lorazepam/ midazolam – don't wait for MRI.*  
- **Intratumoural haemorrhage (especially melanoma/RCC metastases):** Sudden catastrophic headache + vomiting + GCS drop. *Avoid LP – get CT head stat.*

### Presentation
- **Headache:** Worse in morning, with coughing/bending, new-onset in cancer patient  
- **Focal neurological deficit:** Hemiparesis, aphasia, visual field cut – correlates with lesion location  
- **Seizures:** Focal onset +/- secondary generalisation – up to 50% of primary brain tumours  
- **Raised ICP signs:** Nausea/vomiting, papilloedema, bradycardia + hypertension (Cushing’s triad late)  
- **Cognitive change:** Confusion, personality change, slow thinking (especially frontal lobe)  
- **New-onset epilepsy in an adult:** This IS brain tumour until proven otherwise

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Brain abscess:** Ring-enhancing lesion + fever + sepsis history → differentiate by diffusion-weighted MRI + blood cultures  
- **Stroke (ischaemic/haemorrhagic):** Acute onset <24h vs progressive; CT head without contrast key  
- **Subdural haematoma:** Often chronic, fluctuating consciousness, history of falls/anticoagulation  
- **Meningioma (common benign):** Extra-axial, dural-based, slow growth – often incidental  
- **Primary CNS lymphoma:** Periventricular, uniform enhancement, often HIV+ / immunosuppressed  
- **Metastases from unknown primary:** Multiple lesions, supra/infratentorial; search for lung/breast/melanoma/RCC/colorectal  
- **Splitter:** In a cancer patient with a single brain lesion, the top differentials are solitary metastasis vs brain abscess – use diffusion-weighted MRI (restricted diffusion suggests abscess).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - Blood glucose (fingerstick) to rule out hypoglycaemia causing focal signs  
  - Neurological exam: NIHSS (if stroke mimic suspected)  
  - Visual fields by confrontation, pupil reactivity, fundoscopy (papilloedema if ICP high)  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, calcium, coagulation screen  
  - ESR/CRP (elevated in abscess)  
  - If suspected primary: tumour markers (AFP, β-hCG, CEA, CA125? – but not urgent)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** CT head without contrast (for acute presentation, rule out haemorrhage/hermiation)  
  - **Second-line:** MRI brain with gadolinium (characterise lesion, differentiate abscess, see oedema) – do not delay if stable  
  - Consider CT chest/abdomen/pelvis if no known primary (for metastases)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - EEG if unexplained seizure or non-convulsive status suspected  
  - Biopsy (stereotactic) – definitive diagnosis if single lesion, no known primary, or planned for resection  
  - **Never-miss test:** CT head or MRI brain with contrast in ANY adult with new-onset seizure – failing to image delays treatment of a potentially curable cause.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Secure airway if GCS <8 or signs of herniation  
  - If suspected raised ICP: elevate head 30°, avoid fluids that lower osmolality, give **dexamethasone 10-16 mg IV/PO stat** (reduces vasogenic oedema)  
  - For seizures: **lorazepam 2-4 mg IV** or **midazolam 5-10 mg buccal**; load with **levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV** (max 4-5 g) if in status  
  - Pain management: paracetamol 1 g IV/PO – avoid opioids that mask neurology  
  - **DO NOT give mannitol or hyperventilate unless imminent herniation – only under senior guidance**  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Continue dexamethasone taper (e.g. 16 mg daily in 2-4 divided doses, reduce by 50% every 48h) – monitor for hyperglycaemia, GI bleeding, psychosis  
  - Anti-epileptic drugs (AEDs) only if seizure occurred or at high risk (tumours near motor cortex, melanoma) – levetiracetam 500-1000 mg BD  
  - VTE prophylaxis: **enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily** (risk of haemorrhage? discuss with neurosurgery – many units start 24-48h post-craniotomy)  
  - NBM only if vomiting or awaiting surgery – otherwise encourage oral intake  
  - Arrange MRI brain with contrast, neurosurgical/oncology referral  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Definitive treatment depends on histology: surgical resection, radiotherapy (stereotactic or whole-brain), chemotherapy (e.g. temozolomide for glioblastoma, targeted therapy for EGFR/ALK in NSCLC mets)  
  - For multiple metastases: WBRT or SRS – discuss in MDT  
  - Palliative care referral if poor prognosis or progressive disease  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline 1 L/8h (avoid dextrose if on steroids – hyperglycaemia)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (after discussion with NSx)  
  - Diet: free/puree if swallowing safe; NBM if vomiting or pre-op  
  - Observations: 4-hourly neuro obs (GCS, pupil size, limb power) for first 24h then 6-hourly if stable – escalate if GCS drop ≥2  

### Drugs & Doses
- **Dexamethasone (first-line for oedema):** 10-16 mg IV/PO stat, then 4 mg PO/IV QDS (or 16 mg daily divided). Taper over 2-4 weeks. [Check local policy for taper schedule].  
  - **Key contraindications:** Active GI bleed, untreated infection, systemic fungal infection  
  - **Interactions:** NSAIDs → ↑GI bleeding risk; anticoagulants → ↑bleeding; antidiabetics → hyperglycaemia  
  - **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None significant – but monitor blood glucose (steroid-induced diabetes)  
  - **Most dangerous dose error:** *Abruptly stopping dexamethasone* after >1 week → adrenal crisis – always taper.  
- **Levetiracetam (for seizures):** 500-1000 mg PO/IV BD; loading dose 60 mg/kg (max 4.5 g) if status.  
  - **Key contraindications:** Hypersensitivity  
  - **Interactions:** Minimal – good choice in brain tumour patients (low hepatic metabolism)  
  - **Renal adjustment:** CrCl <50 → reduce dose; check [local policy] for exact CrCl-based dose  
  - **Most dangerous dose error:** *Giving phenytoin as first-line* in brain tumour – risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome, drug interactions with steroids. Use levetiracetam unless contraindicated.  
- **Mannitol (for herniation only):** 0.25-1 g/kg IV over 20 min. [Check local policy].  
- **Lorazepam IV / Midazolam buccal:** as per local guidelines for seizures – beware respiratory depression.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Brain herniation (especially if LP performed with mass effect – fatal)  
  - Seizures (focal or generalised)  
  - Hydrocephalus (if tumour near aqueduct/4th ventricle)  
  - Venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE) – very high risk due to procoagulant state + steroids  
  - Steroid side effects: hyperglycaemia, GI bleed, psychosis, proximal myopathy  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Cognitive decline from tumour or radiotherapy  
  - Radiation necrosis (months-years post RT) – mimics recurrence on MRI  
  - Leptomeningeal disease (seeding via CSF)  
  - Endocrine deficits (if tumour/RT involves hypothalamus-pituitary)  

### Prognosis
- **Metastatic:** Median survival 3-12 months depending on primary and performance status (KPS >70 → longer). Surgical resection of single met + SRS improves OS.  
- **Primary glioblastoma:** Median survival 12-15 months with Stupp protocol (surgery + RT + temozolomide).  
- **Meningioma:** Good – 5-year survival >90% if benign and completely resected.  
- **Incidental low-grade glioma:** Watchful – survival often >5 years; but may transform.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check visual fields** – even subtle hemianopia gives you the lesion side and confirms posterior circulation involvement.  
- **Send urgent MRI brain with contrast** in any cancer patient with new headache + focal neurology – don’t wait for CT.  
- **Prescribe a stool softener** (e.g. lactulose 15 mL BD) when starting dexamethasone – prevents constipation which increases ICP.  
- **Arrange a social work/occupational therapy review** early – brain tumours cause functional disability that may need discharge planning.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT perform lumbar puncture** if there is any suggestion of mass effect on CT – risk of tonsillar herniation is fatal. Always get imaging first.  
- **Do NOT give heparin or LMWH** for VTE prophylaxis without discussing with neurosurgery – risk of intratumoural haemorrhage. If on therapeutic anticoagulation (e.g. for PE), bridge with IV unfractionated heparin only after NSx approval.  
- **Do NOT give opioids for headache** without first imaging – they mask neurology and cause respiratory depression. Use paracetamol + dexamethasone.  
- **Do NOT stop steroids abruptly** – taper over 2-4 weeks to avoid adrenal insufficiency and rebound oedema.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening headache not relieved by paracetamol or dexamethasone  
  - New focal weakness, speech difficulty, or vision change  
  - Prolonged (>5 min) seizure or more than 2 seizures in 24 hours  
  - Vomiting (suggests raised ICP)  
  - Suspected spinal cord compression (back pain + leg weakness/urinary retention)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Neurosurgery clinic within 1-2 weeks (or sooner if pre-op)  
  - Oncology referral for definitive treatment planning (SRS, WBRT, chemo)  
  - If no known primary: urgent CT CAP and referral to lung/breast/general oncology MDT  

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) immediately if:**  
  - GCS drops by ≥2 points  
  - New pupillary asymmetry or non-reactive pupil  
  - Seizure lasting >5 min (status) – call anaesthetics/ICU  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4 mmol/L  
  - Sudden severe headache with neck stiffness (suggestive of SAH from intratumoural bleed)  
- **Call crash team / ICU if:**  
  - GCS <8 (airway compromise)  
  - Cushing’s reflex (bradycardia + hypertension + irregular respirations)  
  - Any seizure in a patient with known raised ICP – may herniate
